name: Late-Game Control Finisher
summary: A high-mana commander that stabilizes or dominates the game after resolving.
recommendation_focus: cards that convert large hands into advantage
avoid_recommendations: small unrelated value creatures
generic combat tricks
theme cards that only match one word of the commander
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the commander is not the early engine.
Prioritize reaching the commander safely.
Emphasize protection and timing around the turn the commander resolves.
Mention that the deck should still function before the commander arrives.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain which resource need the card solves.
Do not claim direct synergy unless the card interacts with the commander text.
For ramp cards, explain that they support the commander's mana requirement rather than its mechanic.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify every expensive commander as this archetype. The commander also needs a meaningful payoff, lock piece, or stabilizing effect.

name: Low-Curve Value Engine
summary: A cheap commander that turns repeated small game actions into sustained advantage.
recommendation_focus: cards that trigger the commander multiple times
avoid_recommendations: expensive splashy cards with no engine role
high-cost finishers that slow the deck down
narrow cards that only match a word
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the commander should come down early.
Prioritize efficient setup pieces that can trigger the commander repeatedly.
Mention that small value compounds over multiple turns.
Warn against overloading the deck with cards that are powerful but too slow.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify the repeatable action that the card supports.
Explain whether the card is an enabler, payoff, or protection piece.
Avoid claiming the card is core synergy if it only shares a card type.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a cheap commander this way unless it actually produces repeatable advantage or reliably enables a repeatable advantage loop.

name: Enchantment Toolbox Engine
summary: A strategy that uses enchantment density, enchantment payoffs, and selective tutoring or recursion to assemble value and control pieces.
recommendation_focus: aura packages when the commander supports them
avoid_recommendations: generic aura combat tricks in non-Voltron builds
artifact synergy cards
creatures that only happen to mention enchantments once
pilot_note_guidance: Explain whether the deck is using enchantments as a value engine, a toolbox, or a finisher package.
Prioritize enchantment density when the commander rewards casting or controlling them.
Mention that removal and protection can be included as enchantments to preserve synergy.
Clarify when aura cards are actually part of the plan versus a false Voltron read.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should say whether the card increases enchantment density, triggers the commander, solves a toolbox need, or protects the engine.
Do not describe every enchantment as a direct combo piece.
For tutors, explain the type of target the card can find.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as enchantment toolbox just because it uses a few powerful enchantments. The commander or deck plan must reward enchantment density, tutoring, or recursion.

name: Aura Voltron
summary: A commander-damage strategy that uses Auras to make one threat evasive, protected, and lethal.
recommendation_focus: ways to recover after commander removal
avoid_recommendations: generic enchantment pillow-fort cards
wide-board token payoffs
equipment packages unless the commander also supports them
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck is trying to win through one enhanced attacker.
Prioritize protecting the commander before committing too many Auras.
Mention evasion and commander-damage clocks.
Warn that overextending into removal is a major risk.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify whether the card provides protection, evasion, damage scaling, draw, or recovery.
Do not call an Aura good solely because it is an enchantment.
When recommending draw, connect it to Aura casting or enchanted-creature attacks.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify every enchantment deck as Aura Voltron. The commander must pressure life totals or commander damage through one enhanced body.

name: Equipment Voltron
summary: A commander-damage strategy that uses Equipment to build a resilient single-attacker threat.
avoid_recommendations: generic artifact value cards with no combat role
wide token payoffs
unrelated Auras unless the commander supports mixed Voltron
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck wants to suit up one primary attacker.
Prioritize Equipment that solves damage, evasion, or protection.
Mention equip costs and tempo as major constraints.
Warn that artifact count alone is not the same as an Equipment plan.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain what combat role the Equipment fills.
For cost reducers, explain how they improve tempo rather than claiming direct damage synergy.
Do not recommend artifacts just because the commander mentions Equipment.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify artifact decks as Equipment Voltron unless the commander and support cards reward attacking with equipped creatures.

name: Artifact Recursion Engine
summary: A strategy that repeatedly reuses artifacts from the graveyard or exile to create value, combo loops, or board presence.
avoid_recommendations: generic graveyard creatures
equipment-only cards without recursion value
artifact cards that are expensive and do not recur well
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that artifacts are resources to be reused, not just permanents to keep in play.
Prioritize cheap artifacts that can be sacrificed or replayed.
Mention graveyard setup and recursion timing.
Clarify whether the plan is value-based or combo-oriented.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should say how the card enters, leaves, or returns from the artifact loop.
Do not label a card as synergy only because it is an artifact.
For sacrifice outlets, explain what artifact resource they convert into value.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify Treasure decks or Equipment decks as artifact recursion unless artifacts are intentionally reused from the graveyard or exile.

name: Artifact Token Economy
summary: A strategy that uses Treasure, Clue, Food, Blood, Map, or Construct tokens as an engine for mana, cards, sacrifice value, or scaling payoffs.
recommendation_focus: cards that convert tokens into cards or mana
avoid_recommendations: creature-token anthem cards
equipment-only packages
generic artifacts with no token interaction
pilot_note_guidance: Explain what the artifact tokens are used for: mana, cards, sacrifice, or board scaling.
Prioritize repeatable token production over one-shot token bursts unless the deck has payoff density.
Mention that Treasure, Clue, Food, Blood, and Constructs can support different versions of the same economy.
Clarify when the deck wants to spend tokens versus keep them in play.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should name the artifact token role the card supports.
Do not treat all token support as relevant if it only buffs creature tokens.
For Treasure cards, explain whether they are ramp, sacrifice fodder, or artifact-count enablers.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as artifact-token economy from one or two Treasure makers. The tokens need to be a repeated resource or payoff axis.

name: Aristocrats Sacrifice Engine
summary: A strategy that converts creatures dying or being sacrificed into drain, cards, mana, counters, or repeatable advantage.
avoid_recommendations: large creatures with no death value
combat tricks
graveyard cards that do not support sacrifice loops
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that creatures are resources to be converted into value.
Prioritize having both fodder and outlets before relying on payoffs.
Mention that the deck wins by compounding death triggers, not necessarily by combat.
Warn against hands with payoffs but no sacrifice outlet or fodder.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify whether the card is fodder, outlet, payoff, or recursion.
Do not claim aristocrat synergy from a creature merely dying incidentally.
For token makers, explain that they provide repeatable sacrifice material.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as aristocrats because it contains black creatures or graveyard cards. It needs sacrifice outlets and death payoffs together.

name: Graveyard Reanimator
summary: A strategy that intentionally fills the graveyard and returns high-impact cards to the battlefield or hand ahead of curve.
avoid_recommendations: small creatures with no recursion value
artifact recursion cards unless artifacts are the target
combat-only buffs
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the graveyard is an extension of the hand.
Prioritize ways to put the right cards into the graveyard.
Mention timing around reanimation and removal.
Clarify whether the deck reanimates large threats, loops value creatures, or recurs permanents repeatedly.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain whether the card fills the graveyard, returns a threat, or is a good reanimation target.
Do not claim synergy from a card merely being expensive.
For self-mill cards, explain what they enable instead of presenting them as generic card advantage.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify every graveyard deck as reanimator. Reanimator needs intentional setup plus cards that return threats or engines from the graveyard.

name: Spellslinger Value Engine
summary: A strategy that turns casting instants and sorceries into repeatable damage, cards, tokens, copies, or other value.
recommendation_focus: cards that reward casting instants and sorceries
avoid_recommendations: expensive sorceries with no payoff role
creatures that do not interact with spells
combat tricks unless targeting matters
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that cheap spells are fuel for the commander or payoff engine.
Prioritize maintaining hand size and mana efficiency.
Mention sequencing spells to maximize triggers.
Clarify whether the deck is value spellslinger, storm, or copy-focused.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should say whether the card is fuel, payoff, protection, or a finisher.
Do not recommend an instant or sorcery just because it has the right card type.
For copy effects, explain what kinds of spells become better when copied.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as spellslinger solely because it runs many interaction spells. The commander or deck needs spell-cast payoffs.

name: Targeting Combo Spells
summary: A spell-based strategy that rewards targeting creatures or permanents, often creating copy chains or explosive one-turn kills.
recommendation_focus: cards that scale with many spell targets
avoid_recommendations: generic removal that cannot target your own plan profitably
expensive spells that break the chain
non-targeted board wipes
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck wants to chain many targeted spells in one turn.
Prioritize cheap spells and card velocity.
Mention protecting the key creature before committing a combo turn.
Clarify that not every combat trick is useful unless it replaces itself or scales.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify the target, trigger, or copy interaction.
Do not call a spell synergistic merely because it is cheap.
For protection, explain that it preserves the key target during a combo turn.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify all spellslinger decks as targeting combo. The commander or payoff must specifically care about targeting or copying targeted spells.

name: Vanilla Creature Matters
summary: A strategy that rewards creatures with no abilities, turning simple bodies into combat pressure, recursion targets, or payoff material.
avoid_recommendations: generic typal lords
ability-heavy value creatures
normal reanimator targets that ignore the no-abilities restriction
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that creatures with no abilities are a real density requirement.
Separate no-ability bodies from normal value creatures.
Mention that payoff cards need enough simple creatures before they matter.
Clarify when recursion is recovering no-ability bodies rather than running a normal reanimator plan.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should say whether the card is a no-ability body or a payoff for those bodies.
Do not recommend normal value creatures as synergy unless they explicitly support the no-ability plan.
For recursion, explain that it returns the restricted creature class rather than any generic threat.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify every creature deck as this archetype. It needs explicit no-abilities text.

name: Lands Value Engine
summary: A strategy that treats land drops, land recursion, and lands entering the battlefield as repeatable sources of cards, mana, damage, or board presence.
recommendation_focus: ways to convert land drops into cards or damage
avoid_recommendations: generic ramp with no land synergy when better land-based ramp exists
creature combat buffs
graveyard cards that do not recur lands
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that land drops are the deck's main engine.
Prioritize cards that create multiple landfall events or recur lands.
Mention sequencing lands after payoffs are on board.
Clarify whether the deck is ramping, grinding value, or setting up a land combo.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain how the card creates, repeats, or profits from land drops.
Do not call a ramp card synergy unless it puts lands into play or directly supports the land engine.
For recursion cards, name the lands or effects they help reuse.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as lands value just because it ramps. The deck needs land-entry, land-recursion, or land-count payoffs.

name: Lands Graveyard Combo
summary: A lands strategy that uses lands entering and leaving the graveyard to generate repeatable draw, mana, sacrifice value, or combo loops.
recommendation_focus: payoffs for lands entering or leaving the graveyard
avoid_recommendations: generic landfall cards that only reward lands entering without supporting recursion
large creatures with no graveyard role
artifact ramp that ignores the land engine
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that lands are reusable resources rather than one-time mana sources.
Prioritize cards that put lands into the graveyard and replay them.
Mention that sequencing land drops matters because the same land can trigger multiple effects.
Clarify how the deck protects or rebuilds its graveyard engine.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain how the card moves lands between battlefield, graveyard, and hand.
Do not treat general ramp as core synergy.
For sacrifice lands, explain what repeatable value they unlock.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify normal landfall as lands graveyard combo unless the deck intentionally uses lands in the graveyard as a reusable resource.

name: Counters Engine
summary: A strategy that repeatedly places, modifies, doubles, or benefits from counters on permanents or players.
recommendation_focus: cards that move or reuse counters
avoid_recommendations: one-shot pump spells with no lasting counters
generic big creatures
proliferate cards with too few counters to multiply
pilot_note_guidance: Explain what type of counters the deck cares about.
Prioritize repeatable counter placement over isolated pump effects.
Mention protecting threats that have accumulated counters.
Clarify when the deck goes tall, wide, or both.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should name the counter type and the card's role in placing or rewarding it.
Do not blur +1/+1 counters with unrelated counter types.
For proliferate cards, explain what existing counters they improve.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as counters because a commander occasionally receives a counter. It needs repeatable counter placement or meaningful counter payoffs.

name: Proliferate Superstructure
summary: A counter strategy that uses proliferate to scale multiple counter types across creatures, planeswalkers, artifacts, or players.
recommendation_focus: cards that place multiple useful counter types
planeswalker or poison support when appropriate
avoid_recommendations: proliferate cards without enough counters to multiply
generic +1/+1 counter cards when the deck cares about other counter types
unrelated infect cards without poison support
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that proliferate needs counters already present before it becomes powerful.
Prioritize diverse counter sources if the deck rewards multiple counter types.
Mention timing proliferate after important counters are established.
Clarify whether the deck wins through creatures, poison, planeswalkers, or value engines.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain which counters the card adds or multiplies.
Do not assume every proliferate card supports +1/+1 counters only.
For poison or planeswalker cards, state why that counter type matters to this build.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as proliferate just because it has counters. Proliferate must be an explicit payoff or repeated scaling plan.

name: Blink ETB Value
summary: A strategy that repeatedly exiles and returns permanents to reuse enter-the-battlefield effects and protect key pieces.
avoid_recommendations: death-trigger cards with no ETB value
reanimation cards unless the deck also uses the graveyard
combat buffs that do not support ETB loops
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck reuses ETB effects rather than relying on raw stats.
Prioritize permanents that produce value every time they re-enter.
Mention using blink defensively to dodge removal.
Clarify the difference between blink, bounce, and reanimation.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify the ETB or blink role the card provides.
Do not call a creature good just because it has an ETB if the ETB is too minor.
For instant-speed blink, explain both value and protection modes.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as blink because it has ETB creatures. It needs exile-and-return effects or a commander that enables repeated blinking.

name: Creature Token Swarm
summary: A strategy that creates many creature tokens and converts board width into damage, sacrifice value, convoke, or anthem scaling.
avoid_recommendations: artifact-token payoffs unless tokens are artifacts
single-creature Voltron pieces
expensive creatures that do not make or reward tokens
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck wants board width.
Prioritize repeatable token production and payoffs that scale with many creatures.
Mention protecting the board from sweepers.
Clarify whether tokens are for combat, sacrifice, or both.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify whether the card makes tokens, rewards tokens, protects them, or converts them into another resource.
Do not recommend token cards that make the wrong token type for the payoff.
For sacrifice cards, explain the token-fodder connection.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify artifact-token decks as creature token swarm unless the deck specifically creates and rewards creature tokens.

name: Typal Synergy Engine
summary: A strategy that rewards playing a high density of one creature type through lords, cost reduction, tokens, recursion, or attack triggers.
avoid_recommendations: strong creatures of the wrong type
generic tribal cards that do not name the correct type
cards that only share color identity
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that creature-type density is a deck-building requirement.
Prioritize cards that are both good individually and match the required subtype.
Mention whether the typal plan is aggressive, value-based, or combo-oriented.
Warn against diluting the type count with unrelated staples.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should name the relevant subtype and why the card supports that subtype plan.
Do not claim typal synergy from a card that only shares the commander's creature type without payoff.
For off-type cards, explain the non-typal role clearly.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as typal solely because the commander has a creature type. It must reward or require a specific subtype density.

name: Attack-Trigger Combat Engine
summary: A combat strategy that generates value when creatures attack, often rewarding wide attacks, extra combats, or doubled attack triggers.
avoid_recommendations: single-threat Voltron cards unless the commander supports them
defensive control pieces that do not support attacking
combat tricks that do not scale with attack triggers
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck wants to declare attacks to generate value.
Prioritize cards that create safe or profitable attacks.
Mention the difference between attack triggers and combat-damage triggers.
Clarify whether the deck attacks wide, tall, or with repeated combat steps.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should identify the attack trigger or the way the card enables better attacks.
Do not confuse dealing combat damage with attacking.
For extra combat cards, explain what triggers become repeatable.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as attack-trigger combat simply because it wins through combat. It needs meaningful triggers on attacking.

name: Combat-Damage Value
summary: A strategy that connects with players to draw cards, make tokens, force discard, goad opponents, or unlock repeatable combat rewards.
recommendation_focus: cards that reward hitting multiple opponents
avoid_recommendations: pure power boosts without evasion
Voltron-only pieces unless commander damage is the plan
defensive cards that prevent attacking
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that connecting with opponents matters more than raw combat stats.
Prioritize evasion and safe attacks.
Mention that damage triggers usually require successful combat damage, not just attacking.
Clarify whether the commander or the whole board needs to connect.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain how the card helps creatures connect or rewards combat damage.
Do not describe attack triggers as combat-damage triggers.
For evasion cards, connect them to enabling the payoff rather than generic aggression.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify every combat deck as combat-damage value. The commander or support cards must reward actually dealing combat damage to players.

name: Goad and Forced Combat
summary: A political combat strategy that forces opponents to attack each other while profiting from combat chaos.
recommendation_focus: cards that profit from opponents attacking
board control that leaves opponents able to fight each other
avoid_recommendations: cards that prevent all combat without a payoff
single-attacker Voltron cards
generic aggro cards that only attack your own opponents directly
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck manipulates opponents' combat rather than simply attacking.
Prioritize staying protected while opponents damage each other.
Mention that goad can create openings but does not replace win conditions.
Clarify when to encourage combat and when to hold interaction.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should say whether the card goads, protects you, or rewards forced combat.
Do not recommend normal combat cards unless they support the political combat plan.
For pillow-fort cards, explain how they redirect pressure away from you.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as forced combat just because it attacks often. It needs to manipulate opponents' attacks or reward goaded combat.

name: Lifegain Payoff Engine
summary: A strategy that repeatedly gains life and converts life total changes into cards, counters, tokens, drain, or alternate win conditions.
recommendation_focus: cards that trigger when life is gained
cards that convert life into cards or damage
avoid_recommendations: one-shot lifegain with no payoff
generic life padding
combat lifelink cards when the deck lacks payoff density
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that lifegain is the trigger fuel, not the win condition by itself.
Prioritize repeatable lifegain over large one-time life bursts.
Mention payoff density and ways to convert life into advantage.
Clarify whether the deck wins through drain, counters, tokens, or alternate win conditions.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain whether the card gains life repeatedly, rewards life gain, or converts life into a resource.
Do not recommend lifegain cards just because they increase life total.
For payoffs, explain what repeated triggers accomplish.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as lifegain because it has incidental lifelink. It needs repeated lifegain triggers or life-total payoff cards.

name: Discard Graveyard Value
summary: A strategy that turns discarding cards into card advantage, graveyard setup, reanimation, madness, or other repeatable value.
avoid_recommendations: random wheel effects with no discard payoff
graveyard hate that undermines the plan
cards that discard as a cost without payoff support
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that discarding is a setup tool or value engine, not just a downside.
Prioritize outlets that can be used repeatedly or at useful timing windows.
Mention which cards are meant to be discarded.
Clarify whether the deck wants reanimation, madness, or hand-cycling value.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain whether the card is a discard outlet, discard payoff, or graveyard payoff.
Do not recommend discard cards that only create card disadvantage.
For wheels, explain whether they refill the hand, trigger payoffs, or load the graveyard.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as discard value just because it has looting. The deck must reward discarding or use discard as a core setup mechanism.

name: Stax, Tax, and Lock
summary: A strategy that slows opponents with taxes, restrictions, resource denial, or lock pieces while advancing its own asymmetric plan.
recommendation_focus: win conditions that operate under the lock
avoid_recommendations: symmetric lock pieces that hurt the deck more than opponents
unrelated value creatures
combat-only finishers that cannot function through the lock
pilot_note_guidance: Explain what resource the deck is restricting.
Prioritize asymmetry so the deck can still function.
Mention that the deck must close games after slowing opponents.
Clarify sequencing: develop mana before deploying restrictive pieces when needed.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain what the card restricts and why the deck can operate through it.
Do not recommend stax pieces generically without explaining asymmetry.
For ramp cards, explain how they break parity under tax effects.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify control decks as stax unless they rely on persistent restrictions or lock pieces rather than normal interaction.

name: Colorless Big Mana
summary: A colorless strategy that uses artifact ramp, utility lands, and colorless mana scaling to cast oversized threats or expensive engines.
avoid_recommendations: colored-mana cards
small artifacts with no ramp or payoff role
generic artifact synergies that do not support big mana
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck's main constraint is producing enough colorless mana.
Prioritize ramp and utility lands before expensive threats.
Mention that colorless lacks some normal interaction and needs specialized answers.
Clarify whether the deck is Eldrazi, artifact big mana, or generic colorless value.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain how the card solves ramp, card draw, interaction, or payoff needs in a colorless shell.
Do not recommend artifacts only because the deck is colorless.
For lands, explain the mana or utility role.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify colored artifact decks as colorless big mana. The deck must have a colorless commander or a clear colorless-mana scaling requirement.

name: Donation and Drawback Abuse
summary: A strategy that gives opponents permanents, temporary threats, or drawback-heavy cards while profiting from ownership, control changes, or delayed consequences.
recommendation_focus: cards that punish opponents for owning or controlling permanents
avoid_recommendations: normal value permanents that opponents are happy to receive
generic reanimator cards without donation payoff
combat tricks that do not help connect or donate
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck weaponizes ownership or control changes.
Prioritize cards that are actively harmful or strategically awkward for opponents to receive.
Mention the multiplayer politics of choosing who gets a permanent.
Clarify timing around temporary effects and end-step drawbacks.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain why the card is useful to give away or how it profits from giving things away.
Do not recommend a card just because it can target an opponent.
For drawback cards, explain how the deck shifts or avoids the downside.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as donation because it has one exchange spell. Donation must be a recurring plan or central commander payoff.

name: Temporary Effect Abuse
summary: A rules-heavy strategy that exploits end-the-turn effects, delayed triggers, temporary control changes, or temporary tokens to keep benefits and avoid drawbacks.
recommendation_focus: cards with delayed sacrifice or exile triggers
avoid_recommendations: normal permanent value cards with no temporary drawback
combat-only cards that do not exploit timing
generic blink cards unless they interact with the delayed trigger
pilot_note_guidance: Explain the timing rule the deck is exploiting.
Prioritize cards whose drawbacks can be skipped, ended, or reassigned.
Mention that sequencing and priority matter more than raw card quality.
Clarify which effects are actually temporary and which are not.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should name the delayed trigger or temporary drawback being abused.
Do not overstate synergy if the card's drawback cannot actually be avoided.
For instant-speed cards, explain the timing window they enable.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as temporary effect abuse because it has temporary tokens or theft once. The commander or deck must repeatedly exploit timing or delayed drawbacks.

name: Flash Reactive Control
summary: A strategy that plays primarily at instant speed, holding up interaction while developing threats or engines on opponents' turns.
recommendation_focus: ways to spend unused mana efficiently
avoid_recommendations: sorcery-speed engines that force shields down
expensive tap-out threats without immediate impact
combat tricks that do not protect or generate value
pilot_note_guidance: Explain that the deck wants to pass with mana open.
Prioritize flexible instant-speed plays.
Mention using unused mana at the last safe opportunity.
Clarify when it is acceptable to tap out for a major payoff.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should explain how the card supports reactive play or uses mana at instant speed.
Do not recommend counters generically without tying them to protecting the game plan.
For flash threats, explain why timing matters.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as flash control because it has counterspells. It needs an instant-speed development plan or commander payoff.

name: Hand-Size Pressure Control
summary: A control strategy that punishes opponents for drawing, holding many cards, or failing to empty their hands while using cards in hand as a resource.
recommendation_focus: cards that convert hand size into damage or control
avoid_recommendations: small aggressive creatures
random discard without payoff
cards that empty your own hand without rebuilding
pilot_note_guidance: Explain whether the deck is growing its own hand, pressuring opponents' hands, or both.
Prioritize cards that keep the controller ahead on resources.
Mention that hand-size payoff decks need enough mana to use the cards they draw.
Clarify whether wheels help or hurt the strategy.
reason_builder_guidance: The reason should connect the card to hand size, draw volume, or opponent hand pressure.
Do not call every draw spell synergy unless hand size matters.
For wheel effects, explain the payoff they trigger or the pressure they create.
false_positive_notes: Do not classify a deck as hand-size pressure because it draws cards. The commander or support cards must explicitly care about hand size, drawing, or opponents holding cards.
